title: Why some neighborhoods raise you better than others
summary: Describe what qualities make a neighborhood a good place to grow up in, drawing on personal observation or examples you know.
topic: Why some neighborhoods raise you better than others
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) arguing or describing why certain neighborhoods give children a better start in life than others. Use at least one relative clause, a conditional sentence, the present perfect for observation, and 'however' or 'although' for concession.
hintStarters: Growing up in a neighborhood where people looked out for each other taught me something that no classroom could have…
Not every child gets to grow up in a safe street, and the difference that makes is something I have seen…
If you asked most adults where they learned their first real lessons about the world, most would point not to a school but to…

hintVocab.meaningVi: trông chừng, quan tâm bảo vệ ai đó
vốn xã hội, mối quan hệ xã hội có giá trị
giới thiệu, cho ai tiếp xúc với
truyền đạt, gieo trồng các giá trị
cách nuôi dưỡng, quá trình lớn lên
không gian xanh, khu vực cây cối
thân thiện với người đi bộ
cộng đồng gắn kết chặt chẽ

sampleText:
The neighborhood where I spent most of my childhood was not particularly clean or well-funded, but it had something that I now understand was more important than either of those things: people who noticed when you were not where you were supposed to be. On almost every street there were adults who had lived there for decades and knew every child by name. If I stayed out too late or wandered somewhere I should not have, I could be sure that someone would mention it to my parents by the following morning. That kind of informal supervision, which no city government ever planned or paid for, gave children like me a structure that kept us safer than any locked gate.

However, not every neighborhood I have known has worked this way. I spent two years in a district of Hanoi where families rarely spoke to their neighbors, and the streets, although wider and cleaner than where I grew up, felt empty in a way that was hard to explain. Children played indoors or in compounds, and the public spaces — benches, small parks, pavements — were rarely used for anything social. Research conducted in cities from Lagos to Copenhagen has found that children who grow up in walkable neighborhoods with mixed uses, where shops and homes and parks sit together, develop stronger social skills and a greater sense of confidence in public space than those who grow up in areas designed only for cars and private property. The physical layout of a place can shape the way children learn to move through the world.

What this means is that a neighborhood raises you whether you notice it or not. The streets you walked alone for the first time, the older person who corrected you in front of others, the argument you witnessed between two adults and tried to make sense of — all of these are forms of education that a curriculum cannot provide. We have spent a great deal of time debating school quality as the main factor in childhood development, and that debate matters. But the conversation about what surrounds the school — the street, the park, the corner shop run by someone who has been there for thirty years — has been quieter than it should be. A child who grows up in a place that functions like a community has already received a kind of education before they open their first textbook.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional for hypothetical questioning
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Cấu trúc 'If you asked most adults where they learned their first real lessons about the world' (loại 2) được ngụ ý trong bài viết — dùng quá khứ đơn trong mệnh đề 'if' và 'would + nguyên thể' trong mệnh đề chính để miêu tả kết quả giả định, không phải sự thật thực tế.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The second conditional pattern 'If you asked most adults...they would point...' uses past simple in the if-clause and 'would + base verb' in the main clause to describe a hypothetical outcome, not a real event.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for accumulated observation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'We have spent a great deal of time debating school quality' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để diễn đạt một hoạt động đang diễn ra liên tục từ quá khứ đến hiện tại — không phải một sự kiện cụ thể, mà là một xu hướng xã hội đang tiếp tục.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'We have spent a great deal of time debating school quality' uses the present perfect to express an ongoing activity from the past to the present — not a specific event, but a continuing social tendency.

sampleTranslationVi:
Khu phố nơi tôi trải qua phần lớn thời thơ ấu không đặc biệt sạch sẽ hay được tài trợ tốt, nhưng nó có điều gì đó mà tôi nay hiểu là quan trọng hơn cả hai điều đó: những người để ý khi bạn không ở nơi bạn đáng lẽ phải có mặt. Trên hầu hết mọi con phố, có những người lớn đã sống ở đó hàng thập kỷ và biết tên từng đứa trẻ. Nếu tôi ra ngoài quá muộn hoặc lang thang đến nơi không nên, tôi có thể chắc chắn rằng ai đó sẽ đề cập điều đó với bố mẹ tôi vào sáng hôm sau. Loại giám sát không chính thức đó, mà không có chính quyền thành phố nào từng lên kế hoạch hay trả tiền, đã mang lại cho những đứa trẻ như tôi một cấu trúc giữ chúng tôi an toàn hơn bất kỳ cánh cổng khóa nào.

Tuy nhiên, không phải mọi khu phố tôi biết đều hoạt động theo cách này. Tôi đã sống hai năm ở một quận ở Hà Nội nơi các gia đình hiếm khi nói chuyện với hàng xóm, và những con phố, dù rộng hơn và sạch hơn nơi tôi lớn lên, lại có cảm giác trống vắng theo một cách khó diễn tả. Trẻ em chơi trong nhà hoặc trong khuôn viên, và các không gian công cộng — ghế băng, công viên nhỏ, vỉa hè — hiếm khi được sử dụng cho mục đích xã hội nào. Nghiên cứu được tiến hành ở các thành phố từ Lagos đến Copenhagen cho thấy trẻ em lớn lên ở những khu phố có thể đi bộ với nhiều chức năng kết hợp, nơi cửa hàng, nhà ở và công viên cùng tồn tại, phát triển kỹ năng xã hội tốt hơn và có sự tự tin hơn trong không gian công cộng so với những trẻ lớn lên ở những khu vực chỉ được thiết kế cho xe cộ và tài sản tư nhân. Bố cục vật lý của một nơi có thể định hình cách trẻ em học cách bước vào thế giới.

Điều này có nghĩa là một khu phố nuôi dưỡng bạn dù bạn có nhận ra hay không. Những con phố bạn đi một mình lần đầu tiên, người lớn tuổi đã chỉnh bạn trước mặt người khác, cuộc tranh luận bạn chứng kiến giữa hai người lớn và cố gắng hiểu — tất cả những điều này đều là hình thức giáo dục mà chương trình học không thể cung cấp. Chúng ta đã dành rất nhiều thời gian tranh luận về chất lượng trường học như yếu tố chính trong sự phát triển của trẻ em, và cuộc tranh luận đó rất quan trọng. Nhưng cuộc trò chuyện về những gì bao quanh trường học — con phố, công viên, cửa hàng ở góc phố do ai đó đã ở đây ba mươi năm điều hành — đã yên lặng hơn mức đáng lẽ phải vậy. Một đứa trẻ lớn lên ở nơi hoạt động như một cộng đồng đã nhận được một loại giáo dục trước khi mở cuốn sách giáo khoa đầu tiên.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause correctly?
options: There were adults who had lived there for decades and knew every child by name.
There were adults which had lived there for decades and knew every child by name.
There were adults who they had lived there for decades and knew every child by name.
There were adults that they had lived there for decades and knew every child by name.
mcQuestions.explanation: Use 'who' for people in relative clauses; do not repeat the subject with 'they'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Who' introduces a relative clause for people; no repeated subject pronoun is needed.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the second conditional?
options: If you asked most adults, they would point to the street, not the school.
If you ask most adults, they would point to the street, not the school.
If you asked most adults, they will point to the street, not the school.
If you asked most adults, they would pointed to the street, not the school.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional: past simple in the if-clause + 'would + base verb' in the main clause.
mcQuestions.hint: Past simple in the if-clause; 'would + base verb' in the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'however' correctly to introduce a contrast?
options: However, not every neighborhood I have known has worked this way.
However not every neighborhood I have known has worked this way.
Not every neighborhood, however I have known, has worked this way.
Not every neighborhood I have known has worked however this way.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'However' used as a sentence adverb needs a comma after it at the start of a sentence.
mcQuestions.hint: When 'however' starts a sentence as a contrast adverb, it is followed by a comma.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the present perfect for ongoing observation?
options: We have spent a great deal of time debating school quality.
We have spend a great deal of time debating school quality.
We spent a great deal of time debating school quality.
We have been spending great deal of time debating school quality.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect = 'have/has + past participle'; 'spent' is the past participle of 'spend'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Spend' is irregular; past participle is 'spent'. Use present perfect for ongoing situations.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'although' for concession?
options: The streets, although wider and cleaner, felt empty in a way that was hard to explain.
The streets, although wider and cleaner but felt empty in a way that was hard to explain.
The streets, although they are wider and cleaner, felt empty in a way that was hard to explain.
The streets although wider and cleaner, felt empty in a way that was hard to explain.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + adjective phrase' can be inserted as a mid-sentence concession between commas without repeating the subject.
mcQuestions.hint: A short 'although + adjective' concession can appear in the middle of a sentence between commas.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'shape' correctly as a verb?
options: The physical layout of a place can shape the way children learn to move through the world.
The physical layout of a place can shapes the way children learn to move through the world.
The physical layout of a place can shaping the way children learn to move through the world.
The physical layout of a place can to shape the way children learn to move through the world.
mcQuestions.explanation: After a modal verb ('can'), always use the base form of the verb without '-s' or '-ing'.
mcQuestions.hint: After modal verbs ('can', 'will', 'would'), use the base verb form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'look out for' correctly?
options: The neighbors looked out for every child on the street.
The neighbors looked out after every child on the street.
The neighbors looked over for every child on the street.
The neighbors looked for out every child on the street.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Look out for' means to watch and take care of someone; 'look out after' and 'look over for' are not standard phrases.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Look out for' = to watch and protect; the particle 'out' and preposition 'for' stay together.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'a sense of' before a noun?
options: Children develop a greater sense of confidence in public space.
Children develop a greater sense of confident in public space.
Children develop a greater sense confidence in public space.
Children develop a greater sense for confidence in public space.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A sense of + noun' is the fixed structure; 'confident' is an adjective, not a noun, so 'confidence' is needed.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'a sense of', use a noun, not an adjective: 'confidence', not 'confident'.

options: A child who grows up in a place that functions like a community has already received a kind of education.
A child who grows up in a place that functions as like a community has already received a kind of education.
A child who grows up in a place that is functioning like a community has already received a kind of education.
A child who grows up in a place that function like a community has already received a kind of education.
mcQuestions.explanation: With a relative clause subject 'that', the verb must agree (functions, not function). 'Functions like' expresses resemblance.
mcQuestions.hint: The relative pronoun 'that' refers to 'a place' (singular), so use 'functions' (third person singular).

options: Growing up there exposed me to a wide variety of people and situations.
Growing up there exposed me with a wide variety of people and situations.
Growing up there exposed me at a wide variety of people and situations.
Growing up there exposed me in a wide variety of people and situations.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Expose someone to' is the correct collocation; 'with', 'at', and 'in' are all incorrect here.
mcQuestions.hint: The correct preposition after 'expose someone' is 'to'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'a great deal of' correctly?
options: We have spent a great deal of time debating school quality.
We have spent a great amount of time debating school quality.
We have spent a great deal time debating school quality.
We have spent great deal of time debating school quality.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A great deal of' is the fixed phrase meaning 'a large amount of'; the article 'a' cannot be dropped.
mcQuestions.hint: 'A great deal of + uncountable noun' is the fixed structure; do not drop the article.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If the physical design of a neighborhood shapes how children develop, who should be responsible for creating better environments — local governments, urban planners, residents, or all three?
